title: Library of Congress

infobox.value: 101 [Independence Ave.](/wiki/Independence_Avenue_(Washington,_D.C.) "Independence Avenue (Washington, D.C.)"), S.E., Washington, D.C., U.S.
[Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), citizens, and international visitors

(**LC** or sometimes **LOC**) is a [research library](/wiki/Research_library "Research library") in Washington, D.C., serving as the library and research service for the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") and the *de facto* [national library](/wiki/National_library "National library") of the United States.[[3]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica:_Library_of_Congress-4) It also administers [copyright law](/wiki/Copyright_law_of_the_United_States "Copyright law of the United States") through the [United States Copyright Office](/wiki/United_States_Copyright_Office "United States Copyright Office"), and it houses the [Congressional Research Service](/wiki/Congressional_Research_Service "Congressional Research Service").

The **Library of Congress** (**LC** or sometimes **LOC**) is a [research library](/wiki/Research_library "Research library") in Washington, D.C., serving as the library and research service for the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") and the *de facto* [national library](/wiki/National_library "National library") of the United States.[[3]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica:_Library_of_Congress-4) It also administers [copyright law](/wiki/Copyright_law_of_the_United_States "Copyright law of the United States") through the [United States Copyright Office](/wiki/United_States_Copyright_Office "United States Copyright Office"), and it houses the [Congressional Research Service](/wiki/Congressional_Research_Service "Congressional Research Service").

Founded in 1800, the Library of Congress is the oldest [federal cultural institution](/wiki/Cultural_policy_of_the_United_States "Cultural policy of the United States") in the United States.[[4]](#cite_note-Fascinating_Facts-5) It is housed in three buildings on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill"), adjacent to the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol"), along with the [National Audio-Visual Conservation Center](/wiki/National_Audio-Visual_Conservation_Center "National Audio-Visual Conservation Center") in [Culpeper, Virginia](/wiki/Culpeper,_Virginia "Culpeper, Virginia"), and additional storage facilities at [Fort George G. Meade](/wiki/Fort_Meade "Fort Meade") and Cabin Branch in [Hyattsville, Maryland](/wiki/Hyattsville,_Maryland "Hyattsville, Maryland").[[5]](#cite_note-GeneralInfo-6) The library's functions are overseen by the [librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress"), and its buildings are maintained by the [architect of the Capitol](/wiki/Architect_of_the_Capitol "Architect of the Capitol"). The LC is one of the [largest libraries in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_libraries "List of largest libraries"),[[3]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica:_Library_of_Congress-4)[[6]](#cite_note-FFStats-7) containing approximately 173 million items and employing over 3,000 staff. Its collections are "universal, not limited by subject, format, or national boundary, and include research materials from all parts of the world and in more than 470 languages".[[4]](#cite_note-Fascinating_Facts-5)

When Congress moved to Washington in November 1800, a small congressional library was housed in the Capitol. Much of the original collection was lost during the 1814 [burning of Washington](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington") by British forces during the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"). Congress accepted former president [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson")'s offer to sell his entire personal collection of 6,487 books to restore the library. The collection grew slowly and suffered another major fire in 1851, which destroyed two-thirds of Jefferson's original books.

The Library of Congress faced space shortages, understaffing, and lack of funding, until the American Civil War increased the importance of legislative research to meet the demands of a growing federal government.[[7]](#cite_note-8)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] In 1870, the library gained the right to receive two copies of every [copyrightable work](/wiki/Copyright_law_of_the_United_States "Copyright law of the United States") printed in the United States; it also built its collections through acquisitions and donations. Between 1890 and 1897, a new library building, now the [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building"), was constructed. Two additional buildings, the [John Adams Building](/wiki/John_Adams_Building "John Adams Building") (opened in 1939) and the [James Madison Memorial Building](/wiki/James_Madison_Memorial_Building "James Madison Memorial Building") (opened in 1980), were later added.

The LC's Congressional Research Service provides objective non-partisan research to Congress to assist it in passing legislation. The library is open to the public for research, although only members of Congress, their staff, and library employees may borrow materials for use outside the library.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![indigo progress construction photographs of the Library of Congress Jefferson Building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Loc_contruction.jpg/250px-Loc_contruction.jpg)](/wiki/File:Loc_contruction.jpg)

A series of photographs depicting the construction of the [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building") between 1888 and 1894

### 1800–1851: Origin and Jefferson's contribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1800–1851: Origin and Jefferson's contribution")]

In 1783, [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"), a [Founding Father](/wiki/Founding_Father_of_the_United_States "Founding Father of the United States") and the nation's fourth president, proposed creating a congressional library, but failed to gain necessary support for the idea. After the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), however, the Philadelphia Library Company and [New York Society Library](/wiki/New_York_Society_Library "New York Society Library") served as surrogate congressional libraries when Congress convened in those cities.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-10)

On April 24, 1800, the Library of Congress was established when [John Adams](/wiki/John_Adams "John Adams"), the nation's second president, signed an [act of Congress](/wiki/Act_of_Congress "Act of Congress"), which appropriated $5,000 "for the purchase of such books as may be necessary for the use of Congress...and for fitting up a suitable apartment for containing them."[[10]](#cite_note-11) Books were ordered from London, forming a collection of 740 books and three maps housed in the new [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol").[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

Adams' successor as U.S. president, [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson"), also played a crucial role in shaping development of the Library of Congress. On January 26, 1802, Jefferson signed a bill allowing the president to appoint the [librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress") and establishing a [Joint Committee on the Library](/wiki/Joint_Committee_on_the_Library "Joint Committee on the Library") to oversee it. The law also extended borrowing privileges to the president and vice president.[[12]](#cite_note-13)[[13]](#cite_note-:2-14)

In August 1814, British forces occupied Washington and, in retaliation for American acts of destruction in Canada, [burned](/wiki/Burning_of_Washington "Burning of Washington") several [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States") buildings. Among the buildings burnt was the Library of Congress, which saw over 3,000 of its volumes destroyed.[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12) These volumes were held in the Senate wing of the Capitol; one surviving volume was a government account book from 1810.[[13]](#cite_note-:2-14)[[14]](#cite_note-HistoryBurn-15)[[15]](#cite_note-16) The volume was taken by British Admiral [George Cockburn](/wiki/Sir_George_Cockburn,_10th_Baronet "Sir George Cockburn, 10th Baronet") as a souvenir, and was later returned to the U.S. in 1940 by his family.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

Within a month, Jefferson offered to sell his large personal library[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) as a replacement. He had reconstituted his own collection after losing part of it to a fire. Congress accepted the offer in January 1815, appropriating $23,950 to purchase his 6,487 books.[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12) Some House members, including New Hampshire representative [Daniel Webster](/wiki/Daniel_Webster "Daniel Webster"), opposed the purchase, wanting to exclude "books of an atheistical, irreligious, and immoral tendency".[[20]](#cite_note-:3-21)

Jefferson's collection, gathered over 50 years, covered various subjects and languages, including topics not typically found in a legislative library.[[9]](#cite_note-:4-10) He believed all subjects had a place in the Library of Congress, stating:

> I do not know that it contains any branch of science which Congress would wish to exclude from their collection; there is, in fact, no subject to which a Member of Congress may not have occasion to refer.[[20]](#cite_note-:3-21)

Jefferson's library was a working collection for a scholar, not for display. It doubled the size of the original library, transforming it from a specialist's library to a more general one.[[21]](#cite_note-22) He organized his books based on [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon "Francis Bacon")'s [organization of knowledge](/wiki/Works_by_Francis_Bacon#Scientific_works "Works by Francis Bacon"), grouping them into Memory, Reason, and Imagination with 44 subdivisions.[[22]](#cite_note-23) The library used this scheme until the late 19th century when librarian [Herbert Putnam](/wiki/Herbert_Putnam "Herbert Putnam") introduced the [Library of Congress Classification](/wiki/Library_of_Congress_Classification "Library of Congress Classification"), now applying to over 138 million items.

A February 24, 1824, report from the [Committee of Ways and Means](/wiki/Committee_of_Ways_and_Means "Committee of Ways and Means") recommended a $5,000 appropriation for the Library of Congress, noting the need to improve its collections in "Law, Politics, Commerce, History, and Geography," which were crucial for Congress.[[23]](#cite_note-gi-24)

### 1851–1865: Weakening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1851–1865: Weakening")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/The_Library_of_Congress_in_the_U.S._Capitol_Building_LCCN2010649528.jpg/250px-The_Library_of_Congress_in_the_U.S._Capitol_Building_LCCN2010649528.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Library_of_Congress_in_the_U.S._Capitol_Building_LCCN2010649528.jpg)

The Library of Congress, then located in the [Capitol Building](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") in 1853

On December 24, 1851, the largest fire in the library's history destroyed 35,000 books, two-thirds of the library's collection, and two-thirds of [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson")'s original transfer. Congress appropriated $168,700 to replace the lost books in 1852 but not to acquire new materials.[[24]](#cite_note-Library_of_Congress-25) (By 2008, the librarians of Congress had found replacements for all but 300 of the works that had been documented as being in Jefferson's original collection.[[25]](#cite_note-26)) This marked the start of a conservative period in the library's administration by librarian [John Silva Meehan](/wiki/John_Silva_Meehan "John Silva Meehan") and joint committee chairman [James A. Pearce](/wiki/James_Pearce_(American_politician) "James Pearce (American politician)"), who restricted the library's activities.[[24]](#cite_note-Library_of_Congress-25) Meehan and Pearce's views about a restricted scope for the Library of Congress reflected those shared by members of Congress.

While Meehan was a librarian, he supported and perpetuated the notion that "the congressional library should play a limited role on the national scene and that its collections, by and large, should emphasize American materials of obvious use to the U.S. Congress."[[26]](#cite_note-27) In 1859, Congress transferred the library's public document distribution activities to the [Department of the Interior](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_the_Interior "United States Department of the Interior") and its international book exchange program to the [Department of State](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_State "United States Department of State").[[27]](#cite_note-28)

During the 1850s, [Smithsonian Institution](/wiki/Smithsonian_Institution "Smithsonian Institution") librarian [Charles Coffin Jewett](/wiki/Charles_Coffin_Jewett "Charles Coffin Jewett") aggressively tried to develop the Smithsonian as the United States national library. His efforts were rejected by Smithsonian secretary [Joseph Henry](/wiki/Joseph_Henry "Joseph Henry"), who advocated a focus on scientific research and publication.[[28]](#cite_note-29) To reinforce his intentions for the Smithsonian, Henry established laboratories, developed a robust physical sciences library, and started the *[Smithsonian Contributions to Knowledge](/wiki/Smithsonian_Contributions_to_Knowledge "Smithsonian Contributions to Knowledge")*, the first of many publications intended to disseminate research results.[[29]](#cite_note-30) For Henry, the Library of Congress was the obvious choice as the national library. Unable to resolve the conflict, Henry dismissed Jewett in July 1854.

In 1865, the Smithsonian building, also called the Castle due to its Norman architectural style, was severely damaged by fire. This incident presented Henry with an opportunity related to the Smithsonian's non-scientific library. Around this time, the Library of Congress was planning to build and relocate to the new [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building"), designed to be fireproof.[[30]](#cite_note-31) Authorized by an act of Congress, Henry transferred the Smithsonian's non-scientific library of 40,000 volumes to the Library of Congress in 1866.[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In 1861, President [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") appointed [John G. Stephenson](/wiki/John_G._Stephenson "John G. Stephenson") as [Librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress"); the appointment is regarded as the most political to date.[[32]](#cite_note-John_G_Stephenson-33) Stephenson was a physician and spent equal time serving as librarian and as a physician in the [Union Army](/wiki/Union_Army "Union Army"). He could manage this division of interest because he hired [Ainsworth Rand Spofford](/wiki/Ainsworth_Rand_Spofford "Ainsworth Rand Spofford") as his assistant.[[32]](#cite_note-John_G_Stephenson-33) Despite his new job, Stephenson focused on the war. Three weeks into his term as Librarian of Congress, he left Washington, D.C., to serve as a volunteer [aide-de-camp](/wiki/Aide-de-camp "Aide-de-camp") at the battles of [Chancellorsville](/wiki/Battle_of_Chancellorsville "Battle of Chancellorsville") and [Gettysburg](/wiki/Battle_of_Gettysburg "Battle of Gettysburg") during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War").[[32]](#cite_note-John_G_Stephenson-33) Stephenson's hiring of Spofford, who directed the library in his absence, may have been his most significant achievement.[[32]](#cite_note-John_G_Stephenson-33)

### 1865–1897: Spofford's expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1865–1897: Spofford's expansion")]

[![Library of Congress stacks in the Capitol building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Library_of_Congress%2C_showing_three_levels_crowded_with_stacks_of_books_and_newspapers_LCCN2017646700.jpg/250px-Library_of_Congress%2C_showing_three_levels_crowded_with_stacks_of_books_and_newspapers_LCCN2017646700.jpg)](/wiki/File:Library_of_Congress,_showing_three_levels_crowded_with_stacks_of_books_and_newspapers_LCCN2017646700.jpg)

Library of Congress in the Capitol Building in the 1890s

Librarian [Ainsworth Rand Spofford](/wiki/Ainsworth_Rand_Spofford "Ainsworth Rand Spofford"), who directed the Library of Congress from 1865 to 1897, built broad bipartisan support to develop it as a national library and a legislative resource.[[33]](#cite_note-34)[[34]](#cite_note-35) He was aided by expansion of the federal government after the war and a favorable political climate. He began comprehensively collecting [Americana](/wiki/Americana_(culture) "Americana (culture)") and [American literature](/wiki/American_literature "American literature"), led the construction of a new building to house the library, and transformed the librarian of Congress position into one of strength and independence. Between 1865 and 1870, Congress appropriated funds for the construction of the [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building"), placed all [copyright registration](/wiki/Copyright_registration "Copyright registration") and [deposit](/wiki/Copyright_deposit "Copyright deposit") activities under the library's control, and restored the international book exchange. The library also acquired the vast libraries of the Smithsonian and of historian [Peter Force](/wiki/Peter_Force "Peter Force"), strengthening its scientific and Americana collections significantly. By 1876, the Library of Congress had 300,000 volumes; it was tied with the [Boston Public Library](/wiki/Boston_Public_Library "Boston Public Library") as the nation's largest library. It moved from the Capitol building to its new headquarters in 1897 with more than 840,000 volumes, 40 percent of which had been acquired through copyright deposit.[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

A year before the library's relocation, the Joint Library Committee held hearings to assess the condition of the library and plan for its future growth and possible reorganization. Spofford and six experts sent by the [American Library Association](/wiki/American_Library_Association "American Library Association")[[35]](#cite_note-36) testified that the library should continue its expansion to become a true national library. Based on the hearings, Congress authorized a budget that allowed the library to more than double its staff, from 42 to 108 persons. Senators [Justin Morrill](/wiki/Justin_Morrill "Justin Morrill") of Vermont and [Daniel W. Voorhees](/wiki/Daniel_W._Voorhees "Daniel W. Voorhees") of Indiana were particularly helpful in gaining this support. The library also established new administrative units for all aspects of the collection. In its bill, Congress strengthened the role of librarian of Congress: it became responsible for governing the library and making staff appointments. As with presidential Cabinet appointments, the Senate was required to approve presidential appointees to the position.[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

In 1893, [Elizabeth Dwyer](/wiki/Bessie_Dwyer "Bessie Dwyer") became the first woman to be appointed to the staff of the library.[[36]](#cite_note-37)

### 1897–1939: Post-reorganization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1897–1939: Post-reorganization")]

[![Congressional Library. View from the U.S. Capitol](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Library_of_Congress%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._-_c._1902.jpg/250px-Library_of_Congress%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._-_c._1902.jpg)](/wiki/File:Library_of_Congress,_Washington,_D.C._-_c._1902.jpg)

A 1902 aerial view from the [United States Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol") of the five-year old Library of Congress in its new building that was renamed the [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building") in 1980 in honor of [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson")

[![photograph of west colonnade by Carol M. Highsmith](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Library_of_Congress_and_lawn.jpg/250px-Library_of_Congress_and_lawn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Library_of_Congress_and_lawn.jpg)

[Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building"), built 1890–1897, the Library of Congress's main building, on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill"), Washington, D.C., showing West side colonnade of Jefferson Building, viewed from across First Street and the grounds of the East Front of the [U.S. Capitol](/wiki/United_States_Capitol "United States Capitol")

With this support and the 1897 reorganization upon moving into its new home, the Library of Congress began to grow and develop more rapidly. Librarian Spofford's successor [John Russell Young](/wiki/John_Russell_Young "John Russell Young") overhauled the library's bureaucracy, used his connections as a former diplomat to acquire more materials from around the world, and established the library's first assistance programs for the blind and physically disabled, with the establishment of the [National Library Service for the Blind and Print Disabled](/wiki/National_Library_Service_for_the_Blind_and_Print_Disabled "National Library Service for the Blind and Print Disabled").

Librarian Young's successor [Herbert Putnam](/wiki/Herbert_Putnam "Herbert Putnam") held the office for forty years of the 20th century from 1899 to 1939. Two years after he took office, the library became the first in the United States to hold one million volumes.[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12) Putnam focused his efforts to make the library more accessible and useful for the public and for other libraries. He instituted the [interlibrary loan](/wiki/Interlibrary_loan "Interlibrary loan") service, transforming the Library of Congress into what he referred to as a "library of last resort".[[37]](#cite_note-interlibrary_loan-38) Putnam also expanded library access to "scientific investigators and duly qualified individuals", and began publishing [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_sources "Primary sources") for the benefit of scholars.[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

During Putnam's tenure, the library broadened the diversity of its acquisitions. In 1903, Putnam persuaded President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") to use an executive order to transfer the papers of the [Founding Fathers](/wiki/Founding_Fathers_of_the_United_States "Founding Fathers of the United States") from the State Department to the Library of Congress.

Putnam also expanded foreign acquisitions, including the 1904 purchase of a 4,000-volume library of Indica, the 1906 purchase of G. V. Yudin's 80,000-volume Russian library, the 1908 Schatz collection of early opera [librettos](/wiki/Libretto "Libretto"), and the early 1930s purchase of the Russian Imperial Collection, consisting of 2,600 volumes from the library of the [Romanov family](/wiki/Romanov_family "Romanov family") on a variety of topics. Collections of [Hebraica](/wiki/Hebraica "Hebraica"), Chinese, and Japanese works were also acquired. On one occasion, Congress initiated an acquisition: in 1929 Congressman [Ross Collins](/wiki/Ross_Collins "Ross Collins") (D-Mississippi) gained approval for the library to purchase [Otto Vollbehr](/wiki/Otto_Vollbehr "Otto Vollbehr")'s collection of [incunabula](/wiki/Incunabula "Incunabula") for $1.5 million. This collection included one of three remaining perfect [vellum](/wiki/Vellum "Vellum") copies of the [Gutenberg Bible](/wiki/Gutenberg_Bible "Gutenberg Bible").[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)[[38]](#cite_note-39)

[![Gutenberg Bible on display at the Library of Congress](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Gutenberg_Bible.jpg/250px-Gutenberg_Bible.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gutenberg_Bible.jpg)

[Gutenberg Bible](/wiki/Gutenberg_Bible "Gutenberg Bible") on display at the Library of Congress

Putnam established the [Legislative Reference Service](/wiki/Legislative_Reference_Service "Legislative Reference Service") (LRS) in 1914 as a separative administrative unit of the library. Based on the [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era")'s philosophy of science to be used to solve problems, and modeled after successful research branches of state legislatures, the LRS would provide informed answers to Congressional research inquiries on almost any topic. Congress passed in 1925 an act allowing the Library of Congress to establish a trust fund board to accept donations and endowments, giving the library a role as a [patron of the arts](/wiki/Patron_of_the_arts "Patron of the arts"). The library received donations and endowments by such prominent wealthy individuals as [John D. Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller"), James B. Wilbur, and [Archer M. Huntington](/wiki/Archer_M._Huntington "Archer M. Huntington"). Gertrude Clarke Whittall donated five [Stradivarius violins](/wiki/List_of_Stradivarius_instruments#Violins "List of Stradivarius instruments") to the library. [Elizabeth Sprague Coolidge](/wiki/Elizabeth_Sprague_Coolidge "Elizabeth Sprague Coolidge")'s donations paid for a [concert hall](/wiki/Concert_hall "Concert hall") to be constructed within the Library of Congress building and an [honorarium](/wiki/Honorarium "Honorarium") established for the Music Division to pay live performers for concerts. A number of chairs and consultantships were established from the donations, the most well-known of which is the [Poet Laureate Consultant](/wiki/Poet_Laureate_Consultant_in_Poetry_to_the_Library_of_Congress "Poet Laureate Consultant in Poetry to the Library of Congress").[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

The library's expansion eventually filled the library's Main Building, although it used shelving expansions in 1910 and 1927. The library needed to expand into a new structure. Congress acquired nearby land in 1928 and approved construction of the Annex Building (later known as the [John Adams Building](/wiki/John_Adams_Building "John Adams Building")) in 1930. Although delayed during the [Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") years, it was completed in 1938 and opened to the public in 1939.[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

### 1939–1987: National versus legislative role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1939–1987: National versus legislative role")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Adams_Building_1938_%2831274181753%29.jpg/250px-Adams_Building_1938_%2831274181753%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adams_Building_1938_(31274181753).jpg)

What is now the Library of Congress's second structure of the [John Adams Building](/wiki/John_Adams_Building "John Adams Building") that opened in 1939 on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill") in Washington, D.C.

In 1939, following Putnam's retirement, President [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") appointed poet and writer [Archibald MacLeish](/wiki/Archibald_MacLeish "Archibald MacLeish") as his successor. Occupying the post from 1939 to 1944 during the height of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), MacLeish became the most widely known librarian of Congress in the library's history. MacLeish encouraged librarians to oppose [totalitarianism](/wiki/Totalitarianism "Totalitarianism") on behalf of democracy; dedicated the South Reading Room of the Adams Building to Thomas Jefferson, and commissioned artist [Ezra Winter](/wiki/Ezra_Winter "Ezra Winter") to paint four themed murals for the room. He established a "democracy alcove" in the Main Reading Room of the Jefferson Building for essential documents such as the Declaration of Independence, the Constitution, and *[The Federalist Papers](/wiki/The_Federalist_Papers "The Federalist Papers")*. The Library of Congress assisted during the war effort, ranging from storage of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and the [United States Constitution](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") in [Fort Knox](/wiki/Fort_Knox "Fort Knox") for safekeeping to researching weather data on the [Himalayas](/wiki/Himalayas "Himalayas") for [Air Force](/wiki/United_States_Air_Force "United States Air Force") pilots. MacLeish resigned in 1944 when appointed as Assistant Secretary of State.

President [Harry Truman](/wiki/Harry_Truman "Harry Truman") appointed [Luther H. Evans](/wiki/Luther_H._Evans "Luther H. Evans") as Librarian of Congress. Evans, who served until 1953, expanded the library's acquisitions, cataloging, and bibliographic services. But he is best known for creating Library of Congress Missions worldwide. Missions played a variety of roles in the postwar world: the mission in San Francisco assisted participants in the [meeting that established the United Nations](/wiki/Meeting_that_established_the_United_Nations "Meeting that established the United Nations"), the mission in Europe acquired European publications for the Library of Congress and other American libraries, and the mission in Japan aided in the creation of the [National Diet Library](/wiki/National_Diet_Library "National Diet Library").[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/South_Reading_Room%2C_with_murals_by_Ezra_Winter._Library_of_Congress_John_Adams_Building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._LCCN2007687090.jpg/250px-South_Reading_Room%2C_with_murals_by_Ezra_Winter._Library_of_Congress_John_Adams_Building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._LCCN2007687090.jpg)](/wiki/File:South_Reading_Room,_with_murals_by_Ezra_Winter._Library_of_Congress_John_Adams_Building,_Washington,_D.C._LCCN2007687090.jpg)

The Adams Building – South Reading Room, with murals by [Ezra Winter](/wiki/Ezra_Winter "Ezra Winter")

Evans' successor [Lawrence Quincy Mumford](/wiki/Lawrence_Quincy_Mumford "Lawrence Quincy Mumford") took over in 1953. During his tenure, lasting until 1974, Mumford directed the initiation of construction of the [James Madison Memorial Building](/wiki/James_Madison_Memorial_Building "James Madison Memorial Building"), the third Library of Congress building on Capitol Hill. Mumford led the library during the government's increased educational spending. The library was able to establish new acquisition centers abroad, including in [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo") and New Delhi. In 1967, the library began experimenting with [book preservation](/wiki/Book_preservation "Book preservation") techniques through a Preservation Office. This has developed as the most extensive library research and conservation effort in the United States.

During Mumford's administration, the last significant public debate occurred about the Library of Congress's role as both a legislative and national library. Asked by Joint Library Committee chairman Senator [Claiborne Pell](/wiki/Claiborne_Pell "Claiborne Pell") (D-RI) to assess operations and make recommendations, Douglas Bryant of [Harvard University Library](/wiki/Harvard_University_Library "Harvard University Library") proposed several institutional reforms. These included expanding national activities and services and various organizational changes, all of which would emphasize the library's federal role rather than its legislative role. Bryant suggested changing the name of the Library of Congress, a recommendation rebuked by Mumford as "unspeakable violence to tradition." The debate continued within the library community for some time. The [Legislative Reorganization Act of 1970](/wiki/Legislative_Reorganization_Act_of_1970 "Legislative Reorganization Act of 1970") renewed emphasis for the library on its legislative roles, requiring a greater focus on research for Congress and congressional committees, and renaming the Legislative Reference Service as the [Congressional Research Service](/wiki/Congressional_Research_Service "Congressional Research Service").[[11]](#cite_note-loc_history-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/JamesMadisonMemorialBuilding.jpg/250px-JamesMadisonMemorialBuilding.jpg)](/wiki/File:JamesMadisonMemorialBuilding.jpg)

[James Madison Memorial Building](/wiki/James_Madison_Memorial_Building "James Madison Memorial Building") opened in 1980 [[39]](#cite_note-40)

After Mumford retired in 1974, President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") appointed historian [Daniel J. Boorstin](/wiki/Daniel_J._Boorstin "Daniel J. Boorstin") as a librarian. Boorstin's first challenge was to manage the relocation of some sections to the new Madison Building, which took place between 1980 and 1982. With this accomplished, Boorstin focused on other areas of library administration, such as acquisitions and collections. Taking advantage of steady budgetary growth, from $116 million in 1975 to over $250 million by 1987, Boorstin enhanced institutional and staff ties with scholars, authors, publishers, cultural leaders, and the business community. His activities changed the post of librarian of Congress so that by the time he retired in 1987, *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* called this office "perhaps the leading intellectual public position in the nation."

### 1987–2015 Billington leadership, digitization and programs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1987–2015 Billington leadership, digitization and programs")]

In 1987, President [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") nominated historian [James H. Billington](/wiki/James_H._Billington "James H. Billington") as the thirteenth librarian of Congress, and the [U.S. Senate](/wiki/United_States_Senate "United States Senate") unanimously confirmed the appointment.[[40]](#cite_note-loc.gov-41)

Under Billington's leadership, the library doubled the size of its analog collections from 85.5 million items in 1987 to more than 160 million items in 2014. At the same time, it established new programs and employed new technologies to "get the champagne out of the bottle". These included:

* [American Memory](/wiki/American_Memory "American Memory") created in 1990, which became the [National Digital Library](/wiki/National_Digital_Library_Program "National Digital Library Program") in 1994. It provides free access online to digitized American history and culture resources, including [primary sources](/wiki/Primary_source "Primary source"), with curatorial explanations to support use in [K-12 education](/wiki/K-12_education "K-12 education").[[41]](#cite_note-42)
* [THOMAS.gov](/wiki/THOMAS.gov "THOMAS.gov") website launched in 1994 to provide free public access to U.S. federal legislative information with ongoing updates; and [Congress.gov](/wiki/Congress.gov "Congress.gov") website to provide a state-of-the-art framework for both Congress and the public in 2012;[[42]](#cite_note-43)
* [National Book Festival](/wiki/National_Book_Festival "National Book Festival"), founded in 2001 with First Lady [Laura Bush](/wiki/Laura_Bush "Laura Bush"),[[43]](#cite_note-44) has attracted more than 1,000 authors and a million guests to the [National Mall](/wiki/National_Mall "National Mall") and the [Washington Convention Center](/wiki/Washington_Convention_Center "Washington Convention Center") to celebrate reading. With a major gift from [David Rubenstein](/wiki/David_Rubenstein "David Rubenstein") in 2013, the library established the [Library of Congress Literacy Awards](/wiki/Library_of_Congress_Literacy_Awards "Library of Congress Literacy Awards") to recognize and support achievements in improving literacy in the U.S. and abroad;[[44]](#cite_note-45)
* [Kluge Center](/wiki/John_W._Kluge_Center "John W. Kluge Center"), started with a grant of $60 million from [John W. Kluge](/wiki/John_W._Kluge "John W. Kluge") in 2000, brings international scholars and researchers to use library resources and to interact with policymakers and the public. It hosts public lectures and scholarly events, provides endowed Kluge fellowships, and awards the [Kluge Prize for the Study of Humanity](/wiki/Kluge_Prize "Kluge Prize") (now worth $1.5 million), the first Nobel-level international prize for lifetime achievement in the humanities and social sciences (subjects not included in the [Nobel awards](/wiki/Nobel_awards "Nobel awards"));[[45]](#cite_note-46)
* [Open World Leadership Center](/wiki/Open_World_Leadership_Center "Open World Leadership Center"), established in 2000; by 2015 this program administered 23,000 professional exchanges for emerging post-Soviet leaders in Russia, Ukraine, and other successor states of the former [USSR](/wiki/USSR "USSR"). Open World began as a Library of Congress project, and later was established as an independent agency in the legislative branch.[[46]](#cite_note-47)
* [Veterans History Project](/wiki/Veterans_History_Project "Veterans History Project"), congressionally mandated in 2000 to collect, preserve, and make accessible the personal accounts of American war veterans from [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") to the present day;[[47]](#cite_note-48)
* [National Audio-Visual Conservation Center](/wiki/National_Audio-Visual_Conservation_Center "National Audio-Visual Conservation Center") opened in 2007 at a 45-acre site in [Culpeper, Virginia](/wiki/Culpeper,_Virginia "Culpeper, Virginia"), established with a gift of more than $150 million by the [Packard Humanities Institute](/wiki/Packard_Humanities_Institute "Packard Humanities Institute"), and $82.1 million in additional support from Congress.

[![mural painting entitled Erotica, by George Randolph Bars](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Erotica_at_Library_of_Congress%2C_Washington_D.C._02223u_original.jpg/250px-Erotica_at_Library_of_Congress%2C_Washington_D.C._02223u_original.jpg)](/wiki/File:Erotica_at_Library_of_Congress,_Washington_D.C._02223u_original.jpg)

*Erotica*, mural painting by [George Randolph Barse](/wiki/George_Randolph_Barse "George Randolph Barse") in the library's main building

Since 1988, the library has administered the [National Film Preservation Board](/wiki/National_Film_Preservation_Board "National Film Preservation Board"). Established by congressional mandate, it selects twenty-five American films annually for preservation and inclusion in the [National Film Registry](/wiki/National_Film_Registry "National Film Registry"), a collection of American films, for which the Library of Congress accepts nominations each year.[[48]](#cite_note-49) There also exists a [National Recording Registry](/wiki/National_Recording_Registry "National Recording Registry") administered by the [National Recording Preservation Board](/wiki/National_Recording_Preservation_Board "National Recording Preservation Board") that serves a similar purpose for music and sound recordings.

The library has made some of these available on the Internet for free streaming and additionally has provided brief essays on the films that have been added to the registry.[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) By 2015, the librarian had named 650 films to the registry.[[51]](#cite_note-52) The films in the collection date from the earliest period to ones produced more than ten years ago; they are selected from nominations submitted to the board. Further programs included:

* [Gershwin Prize for Popular Song](/wiki/Gershwin_Prize_for_Popular_Song "Gershwin Prize for Popular Song"),[[52]](#cite_note-53) was launched in 2007 to honor the work of an artist whose career reflects lifetime achievement in song composition. Winners have included [Paul Simon](/wiki/Paul_Simon "Paul Simon"), [Stevie Wonder](/wiki/Stevie_Wonder "Stevie Wonder"), [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney"), [Burt Bacharach](/wiki/Burt_Bacharach "Burt Bacharach") and [Hal David](/wiki/Hal_David "Hal David"), [Carole King](/wiki/Carole_King "Carole King"), [Billy Joel](/wiki/Billy_Joel "Billy Joel"), and [Willie Nelson](/wiki/Willie_Nelson "Willie Nelson"), as of 2015. The library also launched the [Living Legend Awards](/wiki/Library_of_Congress_Living_Legend "Library of Congress Living Legend") in 2000 to honor artists, activists, filmmakers, and others who have contributed to America's diverse cultural, scientific, and social heritage;
* Fiction Prize (now the [Library of Congress Prize for American Fiction](/wiki/Library_of_Congress_Prize_for_American_Fiction "Library of Congress Prize for American Fiction")) was started in 2008 to recognize distinguished lifetime achievement in the writing of fiction.[[53]](#cite_note-54)
* [World Digital Library](/wiki/World_Digital_Library "World Digital Library"), established in association with [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") and 181 partners in 81 countries in 2009, makes copies of professionally curated primary materials of the world's varied cultures freely available online in multiple languages.[[54]](#cite_note-Background_-_World_Digital_Library-55)
* National Jukebox, launched in 2011, provides streaming free online access to more than 10,000 out-of-print music and spoken-word recordings.[[55]](#cite_note-56)
* BARD was started in 2013; it is a digital, talking books [mobile app](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app") for [braille](/wiki/Braille "Braille") and audio reading downloads, in partnership with the library's [National Library Service for the Blind and Print Disabled](/wiki/National_Library_Service_for_the_Blind_and_Print_Disabled "National Library Service for the Blind and Print Disabled"). It enables free downloads of audio and braille books to mobile devices via the [Apple App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS) "App Store (iOS)").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

During Billington's tenure, the library acquired [General Lafayette](/wiki/Gilbert_du_Motier,_marquis_de_Lafayette "Gilbert du Motier, marquis de Lafayette")'s papers in 1996 from a castle at La Grange, France; they had previously been inaccessible.

It also acquired the only copy of the 1507 [Waldseemüller world map](/wiki/Waldseem%C3%BCller_map "Waldseemüller map") ("America's birth certificate") in 2003; it is on permanent display in the library's Thomas Jefferson Building.

Using privately raised funds, the Library of Congress has created a reconstruction of Thomas Jefferson's original library. This has been on permanent display in the Jefferson building since 2008.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

[![mosaic wall decoration Minerva of Peace mosaic by Elihu Vedder](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Minerva_of_Peace.jpg/250px-Minerva_of_Peace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minerva_of_Peace.jpg)

*[Minerva](/wiki/Minerva "Minerva") of Peace*, [mosaic](/wiki/Mosaic "Mosaic") by [Elihu Vedder](/wiki/Elihu_Vedder "Elihu Vedder") in the library's main building

Under Billington, public spaces of the Jefferson Building were enlarged and technologically enhanced to serve as a national exhibition venue. It has hosted more than 100 exhibitions.[[58]](#cite_note-59) These included exhibits on the [Vatican Library](/wiki/Vatican_Library "Vatican Library") and the [Bibliothèque Nationale de France](/wiki/Biblioth%C3%A8que_Nationale_de_France "Bibliothèque Nationale de France"), several on the Civil War and Lincoln, on African-American culture, on Religion and the founding of the American Republic, the Early Americas (the Kislak Collection became a permanent display), on the global celebration commemorating the 800th anniversary of [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta"), and on early American printing, featuring the Rubenstein [Bay Psalm Book](/wiki/Bay_Psalm_Book "Bay Psalm Book").

Onsite access to the Library of Congress has been increased. Billington gained an underground connection between the new U.S. Capitol Visitors Center and the library in 2008 to increase both congressional usage and public tours of the library's Thomas Jefferson Building.[[40]](#cite_note-loc.gov-41)

In 2001, the library began a [mass deacidification](/wiki/Mass_deacidification "Mass deacidification") program, to extend the lifespan of almost 4 million volumes and 12 million manuscript sheets. In 2002, a new storage facility was completed at [Fort Meade, Maryland](/wiki/Fort_Meade,_Maryland "Fort Meade, Maryland"),[[5]](#cite_note-GeneralInfo-6) where a collection of storage modules have preserved and made accessible more than 4 million items from the library's analog collections.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Billington established the Library Collections Security Oversight Committee in 1992 to improve protection of the collections, and also the Library of Congress Congressional Caucus in 2008 to draw attention to the library's curators and collections. He created the library's first Young Readers Center in the Jefferson Building in 2009, and the first large-scale summer intern (Junior Fellows) program for university students in 1991.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

Under Billington, the library sponsored the Gateway to Knowledge in 2010 to 2011, a mobile exhibition to ninety sites, covering all states east of the Mississippi, in a specially designed eighteen-wheel truck. This increased public access to library collections off-site, particularly for rural populations, and helped raise awareness of what was also available online.[[60]](#cite_note-61)

Billington raised more than half a billion dollars of private support to supplement Congressional appropriations for library collections, programs, and digital outreach. These private funds helped the library to continue its growth and outreach in the face of a 30% decrease in staffing, caused mainly by legislative appropriations cutbacks. He created the library's first development office for private fundraising in 1987. In 1990, he established the James Madison Council, the library's first national private sector donor-support group. In 1987, Billington also asked the [Government Accountability Office](/wiki/Government_Accountability_Office "Government Accountability Office") (GAO) to conduct the first library-wide audit. He created the first [Office of the Inspector General](/wiki/Office_of_the_Inspector_General "Office of the Inspector General") at the library to provide regular, independent reviews of library operations. This precedent has resulted in regular annual financial audits at the library; it has received unmodified ("clean") opinions from 1995 onward.[[40]](#cite_note-loc.gov-41) In April 2010, the library announced plans to archive all public communication on Twitter, including all communication since Twitter's launch in March 2006.[[61]](#cite_note-Grier-62) As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit), the Twitter archive remains unfinished.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

Before retiring in 2015, after 28 years of service, Billington had come "under pressure" as librarian of Congress.[[63]](#cite_note-64) This followed a GAO report that described a "work environment lacking central oversight" and faulted Billington for "ignoring repeated calls to hire a chief information officer, as required by law."[[64]](#cite_note-65)

When Billington announced his plans to retire in 2015, commentator [George Weigel](/wiki/George_Weigel "George Weigel") described the Library of Congress as "one of the last refuges in Washington of serious bipartisanship and calm, considered conversation", and "one of the world's greatest cultural centers".[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### 2016–2025 Hayden leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2016–2025 Hayden leadership")]

Appointed in 2016 by President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), [Carla D. Hayden](/wiki/Carla_Hayden "Carla Hayden") was sworn in as the fourteenth librarian of Congress on September 14, 2016. She is the first professional librarian to hold the post since 1974,[[66]](#cite_note-ALA-Hayden-LibrarianOfCongress-2016-67) holding a Ph.D. in [library science](/wiki/Library_and_information_science "Library and information science") from the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"). She was the [CEO](/wiki/Chief_executive_officer "Chief executive officer") of [Enoch Pratt Free Library](/wiki/Enoch_Pratt_Free_Library "Enoch Pratt Free Library") in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland, from 1993 until 2016, and president of the [American Library Association](/wiki/American_Library_Association "American Library Association") (ALA) from 2003 to 2004.[[67]](#cite_note-BaltimoreSun-EnochPratt-ActingCEO-2016-68)[[68]](#cite_note-ALA-PastPresidents-2011-69)[[69]](#cite_note-Jet-ALAPresident-2002-70) She also is the first woman and the first African American to hold the position.[[70]](#cite_note-71)[[71]](#cite_note-72) She continued the work of digitizing as much as possible of the collection and of expanding electronic access to the collection. She initiated programs to modernize the library, expand access from rural areas, and expanding the infrastructure and technological capacity of the library and
assessment of her leadership of the library by the community reflects her expressed goals. The [Association of Research Libraries](/wiki/Association_of_Research_Libraries "Association of Research Libraries") noted Hayden's transformation of the library "into a more open, accessible, and celebrated U.S. institution, while reaffirming its role as the people’s library".[[72]](#cite_note-73) The [American Library Association](/wiki/American_Library_Association "American Library Association") characterized Hayden as a "wise and faithful steward of the Library of Congress – the library she has called our 'national treasure'".[[73]](#cite_note-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Dr._Hayden_and_Ms._Lynda_Carter_in_Wonder_Women_poses.jpg/330px-Dr._Hayden_and_Ms._Lynda_Carter_in_Wonder_Women_poses.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dr._Hayden_and_Ms._Lynda_Carter_in_Wonder_Women_poses.jpg)

Librarian Carla Hayden (left) and [Lynda Carter](/wiki/Lynda_Carter "Lynda Carter") participating in the June 2017 "Library of Awesome" event at the United States Library of Congress that celebrated the role of comics and graphic novels in promoting literacy, as they strike the typical pose of [Wonder Woman](/wiki/Wonder_Woman "Wonder Woman")

In 2017, Hayden announced the Librarian-in-Residence program, which aims to support the future generation of librarians by giving them the opportunity to gain work experience in five different areas of librarianship, including: Acquisitions/Collection Development, Cataloging/Metadata, and Collection Preservation.[[74]](#cite_note-75)

On January 6, 2021, at 1:11 pm EST, the Library's [Madison Building](/wiki/James_Madison_Memorial_Building "James Madison Memorial Building") and the [Cannon House Office Building](/wiki/Cannon_House_Office_Building "Cannon House Office Building") were the first buildings in the Capitol Complex to be ordered to evacuate as rioters breached security perimeters before [storming the Capitol building](/wiki/January_6_United_States_Capitol_attack "January 6 United States Capitol attack").[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78) Hayden clarified two days later that rioters did not breach any of the Library buildings or collections and all staff members were safely evacuated.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

On February 14, 2023, Hayden announced that the [Lilly Endowment](/wiki/Lilly_Endowment "Lilly Endowment") gifted the library with $2.5 million as a five-year grant to "launch programs that foster greater understanding of religious cultures in Africa, Central Asia, and the Middle East".[[79]](#cite_note-:0-80) The Library plans to leverage the donation in these areas:

* Produce a book and a film about [Omar ibn Said](/wiki/Omar_ibn_Said "Omar ibn Said")
* Provide public access to "programs that enhance knowledge about faiths practiced in the regions, including Indigenous African religious traditions, Judaism, Christianity, Islam, and their influence on daily life."[[79]](#cite_note-:0-80)

On May 8, 2025, two days after Hayden had given testimony to the [Senate Committee on Appropriations](/wiki/United_States_Senate_Committee_on_Appropriations "United States Senate Committee on Appropriations") and the [Committee on House Administration](/wiki/United_States_House_Committee_on_House_Administration "United States House Committee on House Administration"),[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-82) via email and without any explanation, she was abruptly fired by [President Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump").[[82]](#cite_note-83)[[83]](#cite_note-84) *[Publishers' Weekly](/wiki/Publishers%27_Weekly "Publishers' Weekly")* characterized Hayden's termination as the "latest blow to professional research and the literary and arts community."[[84]](#cite_note-opdebeek-85)[[85]](#cite_note-86)

No replacement of Hayden has been nominated. Principal Deputy Librarian [Robert Newlen](/wiki/Robert_Newlen "Robert Newlen"),[[86]](#cite_note-TMK-CJ-CK_2025-87) who would have served as interim librarian was fired and Trump named Deputy Attorney General [Todd Blanche](/wiki/Todd_Blanche "Todd Blanche") as acting librarian of Congress and later fired the deputy librarian and copyright office director (Perlmutter and Newlen), appointing senior DOJ officials Brian Nieves and Paul Perkins as respectively, for the interim. This has been interpreted as an attack on the separation of powers.[[87]](#cite_note-88) Perlmutter has sued to dispute the legality of the dismissal,[[88]](#cite_note-89) as the register is appointed by, and responsible to, the [librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress").

## Holdings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Holdings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Great_Hall%2C_Library_of_Congress_Thomas_Jefferson_Building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._View_of_first_and_second_floors%2C_with_Minerva_mosaic_in_background._%28LOC%29.jpg/250px-Great_Hall%2C_Library_of_Congress_Thomas_Jefferson_Building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._View_of_first_and_second_floors%2C_with_Minerva_mosaic_in_background._%28LOC%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Great_Hall,_Library_of_Congress_Thomas_Jefferson_Building,_Washington,_D.C._View_of_first_and_second_floors,_with_Minerva_mosaic_in_background._(LOC).jpg)

The extravagant design of the Great Hall is an example of [Beaux-Arts architecture](/wiki/Beaux-Arts_architecture "Beaux-Arts architecture").

[![photograph of the Great Hall in the Thomas Jefferson building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Library_of_Congress_Great_Hall_angle.jpg/250px-Library_of_Congress_Great_Hall_angle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Library_of_Congress_Great_Hall_angle.jpg)

The Great Hall interior, looking toward the ceiling

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Library_Congress_October_2016-1.jpg/250px-Library_Congress_October_2016-1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Library_Congress_October_2016-1.jpg)

Ceiling of the Great Hall

The collections of the Library of Congress include more than 32 million catalogued books and other print materials in 470 languages; more than 61 million manuscripts; the largest rare book collection[[89]](#cite_note-90) in North America, including the rough draft of the [Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence"), a [Gutenberg Bible](/wiki/Gutenberg_Bible "Gutenberg Bible") (originating from the [Saint Blaise Abbey, Black Forest](/wiki/Saint_Blaise_Abbey,_Black_Forest "Saint Blaise Abbey, Black Forest")—one of only three perfect [vellum](/wiki/Vellum "Vellum") copies known to exist);[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-93) over 1 million [U.S. government](/wiki/U.S._government "U.S. government") publications; 1 million issues of world newspapers spanning the past three centuries; 33,000 bound newspaper volumes; 500,000 [microfilm](/wiki/Microfilm "Microfilm") reels; U.S. and foreign comic books—over 12,000 titles in all, totaling more than 140,000 issues;[[93]](#cite_note-94) 1.9 million moving images (as of 2020); 5.3 million maps; 6 million works of [sheet music](/wiki/Sheet_music "Sheet music"); 3 million [sound recordings](/wiki/Sound_recording "Sound recording"); more than 14.7 million prints and photographic images including fine and popular art pieces and architectural drawings;[[94]](#cite_note-95) the [Betts Stradivarius](/wiki/Betts_Stradivarius "Betts Stradivarius"); and the [Cassavetti Stradivarius](/wiki/List_of_Stradivarius_instruments#Violas "List of Stradivarius instruments").

The library developed a system of book classification called [Library of Congress Classification](/wiki/Library_of_Congress_Classification "Library of Congress Classification") (LCC), which is used by most U.S. research and [university libraries](/wiki/University_library "University library").

The library serves as a legal repository for [copyright](/wiki/Copyright "Copyright") protection and [copyright registration](/wiki/Copyright_registration "Copyright registration"), and as the base for the [United States Copyright Office](/wiki/United_States_Copyright_Office "United States Copyright Office"). Regardless of whether they register their copyright, all publishers are required to submit two complete copies of their published works to the library—this requirement is known as *mandatory deposit*.[[95]](#cite_note-96) Nearly 15,000 new items published in the U.S. arrive every business day at the library. Contrary to popular belief, however, the library does not retain all of these works in its permanent collection, although it does add an average of 12,000 items per day.[[4]](#cite_note-Fascinating_Facts-5) Rejected items are used in trades with other libraries around the world, distributed to federal agencies, or donated to schools, communities, and other organizations within the United States.[[4]](#cite_note-Fascinating_Facts-5)

The legal requirement of mandatory deposit was challenged in *[Valancourt Books v. Garland](/wiki/Valancourt_Books_v._Garland "Valancourt Books v. Garland")*, with the court finding that requiring a publisher to deposit copies of its books at the Library of Congress was a violation of the [Takings Clause](/wiki/Takings_Clause "Takings Clause").[[96]](#cite_note-Reuters2023-97)

As is true of many [similar libraries](/wiki/Legal_deposit "Legal deposit"), the Library of Congress retains copies of every publication in the English language that is deemed significant.
The Library of Congress states that its collection fills about 838 mi (1,349 km) of bookshelves and holds more than 167 million items with over 39 million books and other print materials.[[6]](#cite_note-FFStats-7) A 2000 study by information scientists [Peter Lyman](/wiki/Peter_Lyman "Peter Lyman") and [Hal Varian](/wiki/Hal_Varian "Hal Varian") suggested that the amount of uncompressed [textual data](/wiki/Text_file "Text file") represented by the 26 million books then in the collection was 10 [terabytes](/wiki/Terabyte "Terabyte").[[97]](#cite_note-98)

The library also administers the [National Library Service for the Blind and Physically Handicapped](/wiki/National_Library_Service_for_the_Blind_and_Print_Disabled "National Library Service for the Blind and Print Disabled"), an audio book and [braille](/wiki/Braille "Braille") library program provided to more than 766,000 Americans.

### Digital

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Digital")]

The library's first digitization project, [American Memory](/wiki/American_Memory "American Memory"), was launched in 1990, and was initially planned to choose 160 million objects from its collection to make digitally available on [LaserDiscs](/wiki/LaserDisc "LaserDisc") and [CDs](/wiki/CDs "CDs"), which were distributed to schools and libraries.

After realizing that this plan would be too expensive and inefficient, and with the rise of the Internet, the library decided to instead make digitized material available over the Internet. This project was made official in the National Digital Library Program (NDLP), created in October 1994. By 1999, the NDLP had succeeded in digitizing over 5 million objects and had a budget of $12 million. As of 2022, the library's website contains 914 million unique digital objects, comprising over 21 [petabytes](/wiki/Petabyte "Petabyte") of data.[[98]](#cite_note-:1-99)

American Memory is a source for [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain") image resources, as well as audio, video, and archived Web content.

Nearly all of the lists of holdings, the *catalogs* of the library, can be consulted directly on its website. Librarians all over the world consult these catalogs, through the Web or through other media better suited to their needs, when they need to catalog for their collection a book published in the United States. They use the [Library of Congress Control Number](/wiki/Library_of_Congress_Control_Number "Library of Congress Control Number") to make sure of the exact identity of the book.

Digital images are also available at [Snapshots of the Past](/wiki/Snapshots_of_the_Past "Snapshots of the Past"), which provides archival prints.[[99]](#cite_note-100)
The library has a budget of $6–8 million each year for digitization, meaning that not all works can be digitized. It makes determinations about what objects to prioritize based on what is especially important to Congress or potentially interesting for the public. The 15 million digitized items represent less than 10% of the library's total 160-million-item collection.

The library has chosen not to participate in other digital library projects such as [Google Books](/wiki/Google_Books "Google Books") and the [Digital Public Library of America](/wiki/Digital_Public_Library_of_America "Digital Public Library of America"), although it has supported the [Internet Archive](/wiki/Internet_Archive "Internet Archive") project.[[98]](#cite_note-:1-99)

### Congressional

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Congressional")]

In 1995, the Library of Congress established [THOMAS](/wiki/THOMAS "THOMAS"), an online archive of the proceedings of the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), which included the full text of proposed legislation, bill summaries, and statuses, *[Congressional Record](/wiki/Congressional_Record "Congressional Record")* text, and an index of the *Congressional Record*. In 2005 and again in 2010, the THOMAS system received major updates. A migration to a more modernized Web system, [Congress.gov](/wiki/Congress.gov "Congress.gov"), began in 2012, and the THOMAS system was retired in 2016.[[100]](#cite_note-101) Congress.gov is a joint project of the Library of Congress, the House, the Senate, and the [Government Publishing Office](/wiki/Government_Publishing_Office "Government Publishing Office").[[101]](#cite_note-102)

## Buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Buildings")]

[![Aerial photograph of the Thomas Jefferson Building by Carol M. Highsmith](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Thomas_Jefferson_Building_Aerial_by_Carol_M._Highsmith.jpg/250px-Thomas_Jefferson_Building_Aerial_by_Carol_M._Highsmith.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Jefferson_Building_Aerial_by_Carol_M._Highsmith.jpg)

The [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building") and part of the Adams Building (on the upper right) next to the [Supreme Court Building](/wiki/United_States_Supreme_Court_Building "United States Supreme Court Building") (upper left) on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill")

The Library of Congress is physically housed in three buildings on [Capitol Hill](/wiki/Capitol_Hill "Capitol Hill") and a conservation center in rural [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia"). The library's Capitol Hill buildings are all connected by underground passageways, so that a library user need pass through security only once in a single visit. The library also has off-site storage facilities in [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") for less commonly requested materials.

### Thomas Jefferson Building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Thomas Jefferson Building")]

Main article: [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building")

The [Thomas Jefferson Building](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson_Building "Thomas Jefferson Building") is located between [Independence Avenue](/wiki/Independence_Avenue_(Washington,_D.C.) "Independence Avenue (Washington, D.C.)") and East Capitol Street on First Street SE. Construction began in 1890 with granite supplied by [New England Granite Works](/wiki/New_England_Granite_Works "New England Granite Works"), owned by [James G. Batterson](/wiki/James_G._Batterson "James G. Batterson").[[102]](#cite_note-103) The building opened in 1897 as the main building of the library and is the oldest of the three buildings. Known originally as the Library of Congress Building or Main Building, it took its present name on June 13, 1980.[[103]](#cite_note-104)

### John Adams Building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: John Adams Building")]

Main article: [John Adams Building](/wiki/John_Adams_Building "John Adams Building")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/John_Adams_Building_%2831274182293%29.jpg/250px-John_Adams_Building_%2831274182293%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Adams_Building_(31274182293).jpg)

The [John Adams Building](/wiki/John_Adams_Building "John Adams Building")

The [John Adams Building](/wiki/John_Adams_Building "John Adams Building") is located between Independence Avenue and East Capitol Street on 2nd Street SE, the block adjacent to the Jefferson Building. The building was originally known as The Annex to the Main Building, which had run out of space. It opened its doors to the public on January 3, 1939.[[104]](#cite_note-105) Initially, it also housed the U.S. Copyright Office which moved to the Madison building in the 1970s.

### James Madison Memorial Building

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: James Madison Memorial Building")]

Main article: [James Madison Memorial Building](/wiki/James_Madison_Memorial_Building "James Madison Memorial Building")

[![northeast photograph of Madison Building by Carol M. Highsmith](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Exterior_view%2C_from_corner_of_Independence_Ave._and_2nd_St._Library_of_Congress_James_Madison_Building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._LCCN2007687169.jpg/250px-Exterior_view%2C_from_corner_of_Independence_Ave._and_2nd_St._Library_of_Congress_James_Madison_Building%2C_Washington%2C_D.C._LCCN2007687169.jpg)](/wiki/File:Exterior_view,_from_corner_of_Independence_Ave._and_2nd_St._Library_of_Congress_James_Madison_Building,_Washington,_D.C._LCCN2007687169.jpg)

The [James Madison Memorial Building](/wiki/James_Madison_Memorial_Building "James Madison Memorial Building")

The James Madison Memorial Building is located between First and Second Streets on Independence Avenue SE. The building was constructed from 1971 to 1976, and serves as the official memorial to [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"), a [Founding Father](/wiki/Founding_Father_of_the_United_States "Founding Father of the United States") and the fourth President of the United States.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

The Madison Building is also home to the [United States Copyright Office](/wiki/United_States_Copyright_Office "United States Copyright Office") and to the Mary Pickford Theater, the "motion picture and television reading room" of the Library of Congress. The theater hosts regular free screenings of classic and contemporary movies and television shows.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

### Packard Campus for Audio-Visual Conservation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Packard Campus for Audio-Visual Conservation")]

Main article: [National Audio-Visual Conservation Center](/wiki/National_Audio-Visual_Conservation_Center "National Audio-Visual Conservation Center")

[![photograph of the National Audio-Visual Conservation Center Packard Campus in Culpeper, Virginia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Packard-campus-library-of-c.jpg/250px-Packard-campus-library-of-c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Packard-campus-library-of-c.jpg)

The [National Audio-Visual Conservation Center](/wiki/National_Audio-Visual_Conservation_Center "National Audio-Visual Conservation Center") in [Culpeper, Virginia](/wiki/Culpeper,_Virginia "Culpeper, Virginia")

Founded in 2007 and located in [Culpeper, Virginia](/wiki/Culpeper,_Virginia "Culpeper, Virginia") in [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia"), the [National Audio-Visual Conservation Center](/wiki/National_Audio-Visual_Conservation_Center "National Audio-Visual Conservation Center") is the Library of Congress's newest building.[[107]](#cite_note-108) It was constructed out of a former [Federal Reserve](/wiki/Federal_Reserve_System "Federal Reserve System") storage center and [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") bunker. The campus is designed to act as a single site to store all of the library's movie, television, and sound collections. It is named in honor of [David Woodley Packard](/wiki/David_Woodley_Packard "David Woodley Packard"), whose [Packard Humanities Institute](/wiki/Packard_Humanities_Institute "Packard Humanities Institute") oversaw the design and construction of the facility. The centerpiece of the complex is a reproduction [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") movie theater that presents free movie screenings to the public on a semi-weekly basis.[[108]](#cite_note-109)

## Copyright Act

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Copyright Act")]

Main article: [Digital Millennium Copyright Act](/wiki/Digital_Millennium_Copyright_Act#Anti-circumvention_exemptions "Digital Millennium Copyright Act")

See also: [Librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress") and [Register of Copyrights](/wiki/Register_of_Copyrights "Register of Copyrights")

The Library of Congress, through both the librarian of Congress and the register of copyrights, is responsible for authorizing exceptions to Section 1201 of [Title 17 of the United States Code](/wiki/Title_17_of_the_United_States_Code "Title 17 of the United States Code") as part of the Digital Millennium Copyright Act. This process is done every three years, with the register receiving proposals from the public and acting as an advisor to the librarian, who issues a ruling on what is exempt. After three years have passed, the ruling is no longer valid and a new ruling on exemptions must be made.[[109]](#cite_note-110)[[110]](#cite_note-111)

## Access

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Access")]

The library is open for academic research to anyone with a Reader Identification Card. One may not remove library items from the reading rooms or the library buildings. Most of the library's general collection of books and journals are in the closed stacks of the Jefferson and Adams Buildings; specialized collections of books and other materials are in closed stacks in all three main library buildings, or are stored off-site. Access to the closed stacks is not permitted under any circumstances, except to authorized library staff, and occasionally, to dignitaries. Only the reading room reference collections are on open shelves.[[111]](#cite_note-112)

Since 1902, American libraries have been able to request books and other items through [interlibrary loan](/wiki/Interlibrary_loan "Interlibrary loan") from the Library of Congress if these items are not readily available elsewhere. Through this system, the Library of Congress has served as a "library of last resort", according to [Herbert Putnam](/wiki/Herbert_Putnam "Herbert Putnam"), the [librarian of Congress](/wiki/Librarian_of_Congress "Librarian of Congress") from 1899 to 1939.[[37]](#cite_note-interlibrary_loan-38) The Library of Congress lends books to other libraries with the stipulation that they be used only inside the borrowing library.[[112]](#cite_note-113) In 2017, the Library of Congress began development on a reader's card for children under the age of sixteen.[[113]](#cite_note-114)

## Standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Library_of_Congress&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Standards")]

In addition to its library services, the Library of Congress is actively involved in various standard activities in areas related to bibliographical and search and retrieval standards. Areas of work include [MARC standards](/wiki/MARC_standards "MARC standards"), [Metadata Encoding and Transmission Standard](/wiki/Metadata_Encoding_and_Transmission_Standard "Metadata Encoding and Transmission Standard") (METS), [Metadata Object Description Schema](/wiki/Metadata_Object_Description_Schema "Metadata Object Description Schema") (MODS), [Z39.50](/wiki/Z39.50 "Z39.50") and [Search/Retrieve Web Service](/wiki/Search/Retrieve_Web_Service "Search/Retrieve Web Service") (SRW), and [Search/Retrieve via URL](/wiki/Search/Retrieve_via_URL "Search/Retrieve via URL") (SRU).[[114]](#cite_note-115) The [Law Library of Congress](/wiki/Law_Library_of_Congress "Law Library of Congress") "seeks to further legal scholarship by providing opportunities for scholars and practitioners to conduct significant legal research. Individuals are invited to apply for projects which would further the multi-faceted mission of the law library in serving the U.S. Congress, other governmental agencies, and the public."[[115]](#cite_note-116)
